questions.stem: The professor's lecture was so ( ) that students were on the edge of their seats.
questions.explanation: captivating = 心を奪う/魅了する。catastrophic = 破滅的、catalog = 目録、calibrate = 較正。

questions.stem: Despite warnings, the firm's risky strategy ( ) substantial losses for shareholders.
questions.explanation: incur losses = 損失を被る (定型コロケーション)。induce = 引き起こす、insult = 侮辱、infer = 推論。

questions.stem: The new evidence ( ) earlier conclusions about climate sensitivity, prompting major revisions.
questions.explanation: overturn conclusions = 結論を覆す。overhear = 立ち聞き、overstate = 誇張、overlap = 重なる。

questions.stem: The peace treaty ( ) decades of bitter conflict between the two nations.
questions.explanation: conclude a conflict = 紛争を終結させる。conduct = 実施、confer = 授与、confront = 直面。

questions.stem: The artist's work ( ) themes of memory, identity, and loss with rare emotional depth.
questions.explanation: explore themes = テーマを掘り下げる/探求する。explode = 爆発、exploit = 搾取、expire = 失効。

questions.stem: The chairman's resignation came as a complete ( ) to the board members.
questions.explanation: come as a surprise = 驚きとなる (定型句)。surface = 表面、surcharge = 追加料金、surplus = 余剰。

questions.stem: The judge's sentence was widely seen as ( ) given the severity of the crime.
questions.explanation: lenient sentence = 寛大な判決。linear = 直線的、loyal = 忠実、lyrical = 抒情的。

questions.stem: The new policy aims to ( ) the burden on small business owners.
questions.explanation: alleviate burden = 負担を軽減する。allocate = 割り当てる、altercate = 口論、articulate = 明確に表現。

questions.stem: The committee was ( ) in its support for the proposal.
questions.explanation: unanimous = 全会一致の (committee/vote と頻コロケーション)。

questions.stem: The research findings have ( ) implications for public health policy.
questions.explanation: profound implications = 重大/深遠な含意。prolific = 多産、prosperous = 繁栄、provincial = 地方の。

questions.stem: The economist's analysis ( ) that current monetary policy may be insufficient.
questions.explanation: analysis suggests = 分析が示唆する。subject = 服従、subside = 沈静化、subordinate = 従属。

questions.stem: The new building's design ( ) traditional Japanese architecture with modern materials.
questions.explanation: combine A with B = A と B を組み合わせる。commit = 約束、collide = 衝突、complain = 不満。

questions.stem: Despite intensive efforts, scientists have not yet ( ) the cause of the mysterious disease.
questions.explanation: pinpoint the cause = 原因を特定する。pioneer = 先駆者、ponder = 熟考、propel = 推進。

questions.stem: The construction of the dam will ( ) several rural villages in the area.
questions.explanation: displace people/villages = 人/村を立ち退かせる。disclose = 開示、disprove = 反証、dispatch = 派遣。

questions.stem: The CEO's decisions have repeatedly ( ) shareholder expectations.
questions.explanation: exceed expectations = 期待を超える (頻コロケーション)。excel = 秀でる、excerpt = 抜粋、exclude = 除外。

question_data.passage:
The Quiet Power of Ordinary Conversation

Most human communication occurs in unremarkable settings—ordinary conversations between friends, colleagues, family members. We rarely think about these everyday exchanges with the same seriousness we apply to formal speeches, written texts, or televised debates. Yet research suggests that ordinary conversation may be more important to human well-being and intellectual development than we typically recognize.

Conversation serves social functions that other communication forms cannot fully replace. The casual exchanges that occur during shared meals, brief encounters in hallways, or extended phone calls with old friends weave the fabric of social bonds. People in communities with rich conversation practices report higher rates of life satisfaction and lower rates of loneliness, even controlling for other factors.

Intellectually, conversation can promote understanding in ways that other formats often cannot. In dialogue with another person, we are forced to clarify our thinking, respond to questions and challenges, consider perspectives we might not generate alone. Many people report that they understand topics better after discussing them than after merely reading or thinking about them silently. The friction of genuine conversation produces insights that solitary thought misses.

Digital communication has both expanded and complicated conversational practice. Text messages and social media posts allow forms of exchange that geography and schedule once prevented. Yet these formats often lack the spontaneity, emotional richness, and back-and-forth dynamics of spoken conversation. Some scholars worry that we are losing skills of sustained verbal exchange as more communication shifts to text-based formats.

Reclaiming ordinary conversation requires deliberate effort in our distracted age. Setting aside phones during meals. Making time for unstructured talks rather than only goal-directed exchanges. Cultivating skills of careful listening alongside articulate speaking. None of this is dramatic, but the cumulative effect across many small interactions substantially shapes both individual lives and broader social health.

The importance of ordinary conversation reflects something fundamental about being human. We are social creatures whose minds develop and flourish through interaction with other minds. Technologies that enhance such interaction serve us; those that displace it without adequate replacement may diminish us in ways we have only begun to understand.

questions.stem: What benefits does the passage attribute to ordinary conversation?
choices: Social bonding, higher life satisfaction, lower loneliness, intellectual clarity through dialogue
questions.explanation: 第2-3段落「weave the fabric of social bonds ... higher rates of life satisfaction and lower rates of loneliness ... promote understanding ... insights that solitary thought misses」が根拠。

questions.stem: What concern does the passage raise about digital communication?
choices: Often lacks spontaneity, emotional richness, back-and-forth dynamics; we may be losing skills of sustained verbal exchange
questions.explanation: 第4段落「these formats often lack the spontaneity, emotional richness, and back-and-forth dynamics ... losing skills of sustained verbal exchange」が根拠。

questions.stem: What does the author conclude about technology and conversation?
choices: Technologies that enhance interaction serve us; those that displace it without adequate replacement may diminish us
questions.explanation: 最終段落「Technologies that enhance such interaction serve us; those that displace it without adequate replacement may diminish us」が根拠。

question_data.passage:
The Microbiome and Mental Health

For most of medical history, the microbes living in our intestines were of interest mainly when they caused infections. The trillions of bacteria, viruses, and fungi normally inhabiting the gut were largely ignored by mainstream medicine. Recent research has dramatically transformed this picture, revealing that gut microbes—collectively called the microbiome—influence not only digestion but also, surprisingly, mental health.

The connection between gut and brain is not new. Anyone who has felt 'butterflies in the stomach' before a stressful event knows that emotional states affect digestion. What is new is research showing the reverse direction: gut microbiomes appear to influence mood, anxiety, and even cognitive function. The vagus nerve provides direct communication between gut and brain. Microbes also produce neurotransmitters and other signaling molecules that affect brain chemistry.

Studies in mice have shown remarkable effects. Mice raised without gut bacteria show altered behavior—including increased anxiety—compared to mice with normal microbiomes. Transferring microbiomes between mice can transfer behavioral characteristics; bold mice's microbiomes make timid mice bolder, and vice versa. While these findings cannot directly translate to humans, they suggest that microbiome influences on behavior are more substantial than previously recognized.

Research in humans is more difficult but increasingly suggestive. People with depression and anxiety often show distinctive microbiome compositions compared to healthy controls. Some studies show benefits from probiotic supplements for certain mental health conditions, though results vary. Researchers are exploring whether dietary changes that modify the microbiome could complement traditional mental health treatments.

This emerging field, sometimes called 'psychobiotics,' faces substantial methodological challenges. Causation versus correlation is difficult to establish. Individual microbiome differences are vast. Effects vary across people and conditions. Despite these complications, the basic insight—that mental health depends partly on the small organisms living within us—represents a striking shift in scientific understanding.

Practical implications remain preliminary. Diet, sleep, exercise, and stress management likely all affect microbiome composition in ways that may influence mental as well as physical health. While probiotic supplements have not become reliable mental health treatments, the broader recognition that gut health and brain health are connected has shifted thinking about what 'whole-person' health really means.

questions.stem: How do gut microbes communicate with the brain?
choices: Vagus nerve provides direct communication; microbes produce neurotransmitters and signaling molecules affecting brain chemistry
questions.explanation: 第2段落「The vagus nerve provides direct communication between gut and brain. Microbes also produce neurotransmitters and other signaling molecules」が根拠。

questions.stem: What did mouse studies reveal about microbiomes and behavior?
choices: Mice without gut bacteria show altered behavior (more anxiety); microbiome transfers can transfer behavioral characteristics
questions.explanation: 第3段落「Mice raised without gut bacteria show altered behavior—including increased anxiety ... Transferring microbiomes between mice can transfer behavioral characteristics」が根拠。

questions.stem: What broader shift in scientific understanding does the author identify?
choices: Mental health depends partly on microorganisms living within us; gut health and brain health are connected
questions.explanation: 第5段落「mental health depends partly on the small organisms living within us—represents a striking shift」と最終段「gut health and brain health are connected」が根拠。

question_data.prompt:
TOPIC: Should plastic single-use products be banned?

POINTS to consider (use 2 of 4): - Environment - Convenience - Cost - Innovation

Requirements: 120-150 words.
question_data.sample:
I strongly support banning single-use plastic products. I will support this view with two reasons: environmental impact and innovation incentive.

First, single-use plastics cause severe environmental harm. They take centuries to decompose, accumulating in oceans where they harm marine life and break down into microplastics that contaminate food chains. Annual production runs into hundreds of millions of tons globally, with most ending up in landfills or environments rather than recycling.

Second, banning single-use plastics drives innovation toward sustainable alternatives. Companies forced to find replacements have developed compostable packaging, reusable systems, and improved recycling technologies. Without regulatory pressure, market incentives alone often prove insufficient to drive such transitions, since the environmental costs are not reflected in prices.

In conclusion, despite some inconvenience, the environmental damage and stunted innovation caused by current plastic dependence justify strong restrictions on single-use plastic products. (148 words)

questions.stem: Above prompt — 120-150 word essay.
questions.explanation: 【準1級】立場明示+2ポイント (Environment + Innovation)・反対視点 (convenience) を結論で組込・具体的数値と論証。

question_data.prompt:
TOPIC: Will remote work permanently change the way people live in big cities?

POINTS to consider (use 2 of 4): - Housing - Commuting - Office space - Community

Requirements: 120-150 words.
question_data.sample:
I believe remote work will permanently change life in big cities. I will support this view with two reasons: housing patterns and commuting changes.

First, remote work transforms residential decisions. When workers no longer need to live near offices, they can choose homes based on quality of life rather than commute distance. Some are leaving cities for smaller towns offering more space at lower cost. Cities responding by repurposing former office buildings as residences may avoid decline.

Second, reduced commuting fundamentally changes urban rhythms. Public transit systems built for daily commutes face declining ridership. Restaurants and services that depended on office workers are struggling. Cities are adapting by encouraging local neighborhoods that meet residents' daily needs without long travel.

In conclusion, while not all jobs allow remote work, the substantial shift toward flexible arrangements is reshaping urban housing, transportation, and community life in lasting ways. (149 words)

questions.stem: Above prompt — 120-150 word essay.
questions.explanation: 【準1級】立場明示+2ポイント (Housing + Commuting)・具体例で論証・現代社会変化を多角的に。

questions.stem: My grandmother ( ) tea every morning before breakfast.
questions.explanation: 主語 My grandmother (三人称単数) + 現在の習慣 → drinks。

questions.stem: The train was late ( ) the heavy snow.
questions.explanation: because of + 名詞 = 〜のせいで。because は接続詞 (節を導く)。

questions.stem: This is the most expensive watch ( ) I have ever seen.
questions.explanation: 最上級 + 関係詞 → that が定石 (which も可)。what は先行詞含み、who は人、where は場所。

questions.stem: You can get to the museum ( ) bus or train.
questions.explanation: by + 交通手段 (無冠詞) = 〜で行く。by car / by train / by bus 全て同型。

questions.stem: The teacher told us ( ) talking during the test.
questions.explanation: tell + O + to不定詞 = O に〜するよう言う。

questions.stem: He is interested ( ) ancient history.
questions.explanation: be interested in = 〜に興味がある。前置詞 in 固定。

questions.stem: This problem is too difficult ( ) me to solve.
questions.explanation: too 形容詞 for 人 to不定詞 = 人にとって〜すぎて…できない。意味上の主語は for + 人。

questions.stem: I'd like ( ) a glass of water, please.
questions.explanation: would like + to不定詞 = 〜したい (丁寧)。

questions.stem: The little boy is ( ) of the dark.
questions.explanation: be afraid of = 〜を怖がる。afraid は形容詞 (動詞ではない)。

questions.stem: My sister has been studying English for ( ) ten years.
questions.explanation: over = 〜を超える/以上。over ten years = 10年以上。above は位置/レベル、up は方向、more は単独で前置詞にならない。

questions.stem: I've ( ) finished my homework, but I still have to read for tomorrow.
questions.explanation: already = もう (現在完了肯定文で完了済み)。yet は否定/疑問、ever は経験、never は否定。

questions.stem: The room ( ) cleaned every day by my mother.
questions.explanation: 主語 The room (物) + every day (現在の習慣) + 受動態 → is cleaned。

questions.stem: He is one of the ( ) players in the team.
questions.explanation: one of the 最上級 + 複数名詞 = 最も〜な〜の一人。good の最上級は best。

questions.stem: It started ( ) heavily during the picnic.
questions.explanation: start + 動名詞または to不定詞。raining が正解 (to rained は文法的に不可)。

questions.stem: The coffee was ( ) hot for me to drink.
questions.explanation: too 形容詞 to不定詞 = 〜すぎて…できない。

question_data.prompt:
TOPIC: Should schools have a uniform for all students?

Requirements: 80-100 words with 2 reasons.
question_data.sample:
I think schools should have a uniform for all students. I have two reasons.

First, uniforms reduce social pressure related to clothing. Without uniforms, students may feel pressured to wear expensive clothes to fit in. This creates stress for families and embarrassment for students from less wealthy backgrounds. Uniforms create equality.

Second, uniforms save time in the morning. Students don't need to think about what to wear, so they can focus on getting ready for school. This leads to less stress and more time for breakfast or studying.

For these reasons, I support school uniforms. (94 words)

questions.stem: Above prompt — 80-100 word essay.
questions.explanation: 【2級新形式】立場明示+理由2つ (equality + time saving)・各理由を具体例で補強。

question_data.prompt:
TOPIC: Is using a smartphone too much harmful for young people?

Requirements: 80-100 words with 2 reasons.
question_data.sample:
I think using a smartphone too much is harmful for young people. I have two reasons to support my opinion.

First, excessive smartphone use can affect physical health. Many young people experience eye strain, neck pain, and poor sleep because they look at screens for too many hours. These problems can become serious if they continue for many years.

Second, too much smartphone use can affect social skills. Young people who spend most of their time on phones may have less practice in face-to-face conversation, which is an important skill for school, work, and friendships.

For these reasons, young people should limit their smartphone use. (101 words)

questions.stem: Above prompt — 80-100 word essay.
questions.explanation: 【2級新形式】立場明示+健康・社会面の2理由・各補強・結論で再強調。

question_data.prompt:
TOPIC: Should Japanese students study English from elementary school?

Requirements: 80-100 words with 2 reasons.
question_data.sample:
I think Japanese students should study English from elementary school. I have two reasons.

First, children learn languages much faster than adults. Younger students absorb pronunciation and grammar more naturally. Starting English in elementary school gives them a strong foundation that will help them throughout their lives.

Second, English is becoming essential for many jobs. As Japan's economy continues to interact with global markets, English ability matters more than ever. Students who start early have more time to develop the skills they will need in their future careers.

For these reasons, English education should begin in elementary school. (98 words)

questions.stem: Above prompt — 80-100 word essay.
questions.explanation: 【2級新形式】立場明示+理由2つ (語学習得の年齢 + 将来のキャリア)・各補強・結論。

questions.stem: Yumi can speak three languages ( ).
questions.explanation: 動詞 speak を修飾するので副詞。well = 上手に。good は形容詞、best は最上級、better は比較級。

questions.stem: My parents will ( ) me a new bicycle for my birthday.
questions.explanation: will + 動詞原形 (未来)。buy が正解。

questions.stem: She is ( ) her sister.
questions.explanation: 比較級 + than = より〜。young の比較級は younger。

questions.stem: Where ( ) you born? -I was born in Osaka.
questions.explanation: be born = 生まれる (受動態)。過去なので were。Where were you born? が定型。

questions.stem: Tom ( ) tennis since he was eight years old.
questions.explanation: since + 起点 → 現在完了 (継続)。has played。

questions.stem: Could you ( ) me with my homework?
questions.explanation: could you + 動詞原形 = 〜してくれませんか? help が正解。

questions.stem: What ( ) you doing at 3 PM yesterday?
questions.explanation: yesterday + 特定時刻 → 過去進行形。were doing。

questions.stem: I'm sorry. I'm too busy ( ) you now.
questions.explanation: too 形容詞 to不定詞 = 〜すぎて…できない。

questions.stem: Is this dictionary ( ) or your sister's?
questions.explanation: yours = あなたのもの (所有代名詞・単独で使う)。your は所有格 (名詞修飾)、you は主/目的格、yourself は再帰。

questions.stem: There ( ) any students in the classroom now.
questions.explanation: 主語 students (複数) + 否定 + any → aren't。

questions.stem: My father is good ( ) cooking Italian food.
questions.explanation: be good at + 動名詞 = 〜が得意。

questions.stem: How long ( ) you lived in Japan?
questions.explanation: How long + 現在完了 (継続期間) → have。

questions.stem: The lecture was so ( ) that many students fell asleep.
questions.explanation: 物が退屈させる → 現在分詞 boring。人が退屈 → bored。

questions.stem: By the time he arrived at the station, the train ( ) already left.
questions.explanation: By the time + 過去, 過去完了 (had + 過去分詞)。

questions.stem: It is ( ) that we make a decision today.
questions.explanation: It is 形容詞 that S V → 形容詞 necessary。necessity は名詞、necessarily は副詞。

questions.stem: The road was ( ) by the heavy rain last night.
questions.explanation: 主語 The road (物) + 受動態 (過去) → was damaged。

questions.stem: She insisted on ( ) the bill herself.
questions.explanation: insist on + 動名詞 = 〜することを強く主張する。前置詞 on の後に動名詞。

questions.stem: He works as a doctor, ( )?
questions.explanation: 付加疑問文: 肯定文 + 否定の付加疑問。一般動詞 works → doesn't he。

questions.stem: My mother ( ) cooking now.
questions.explanation: 主語 My mother (三人称単数) + 現在進行形 → is + V-ing。

questions.stem: Tom ( ) to school by bike every day.
questions.explanation: 主語 Tom (三人称単数) + 現在の習慣 → goes (三単現の s)。

questions.stem: This pen is ( ) than that one.
questions.explanation: good の比較級は better (不規則)。

questions.stem: What time ( ) it now?
questions.explanation: What time is it? = 今何時? (時刻を尋ねる定型)。

questions.stem: Look at ( ) cute dog!
questions.explanation: 単数 dog → that または this。Look at で指し示す → that が自然。

questions.stem: My brother and I ( ) good friends.
questions.explanation: 主語 My brother and I (複数) → are。

questions.stem: The committee's recommendations were ( ) by the board, leading to substantial reforms.
questions.explanation: heed = 注意を払う/従う (recommendation と頻コロケーション)。hinder = 妨げる、herald = 告知、harness = 利用。

questions.stem: The senator's ( ) defense of the controversial policy was widely criticized.
questions.explanation: vehement defense = 激烈な弁護。venerable = 尊敬すべき、verbose = 冗長、vapid = 退屈。

questions.stem: The artist's later works show a ( ) departure from her earlier style.
questions.explanation: radical departure = 抜本的な転換。rampant = 蔓延した、ravenous = 飢えた、rebellious = 反抗的。

question_data.passage:
The Mystery of Yawning

Yawning is one of the most universal human behaviors, and one of the most mysterious. Babies yawn in the womb. Animals from fish to mammals yawn. Adults yawn an average of 20 times daily. Yet despite this familiarity, scientists still cannot fully explain why we yawn.

The oldest theory held that yawning increases oxygen intake when the body needs it. This explanation seemed intuitive, but research has largely disproven it. Yawning frequency does not correlate with blood oxygen levels.

A more recent theory suggests yawning helps regulate brain temperature. Yawning increases blood flow to the head and brings cool air through the mouth, potentially cooling a slightly overheated brain. Some studies support this idea: people yawn more in warm conditions and less in cold ones.

Perhaps most intriguing is the social function of yawning. Yawns are famously contagious—seeing or even reading about yawning can trigger our own. Research suggests this contagion is stronger between close family and friends than between strangers, suggesting it may play a role in group bonding.

The full picture remains unclear. Yawning likely serves multiple functions simultaneously, with the relative importance of each varying by context. What seems certain is that this mundane-looking behavior connects to deep biological functions that science continues to unravel.

questions.stem: What is the older theory of yawning that has been disproven?
choices: Yawning increases oxygen intake when needed
questions.explanation: 第2段落「The oldest theory held that yawning increases oxygen intake ... has largely disproven」が根拠。

questions.stem: What does the brain temperature regulation theory propose?
choices: Yawning increases blood flow to the head and brings cool air, helping cool an overheated brain
questions.explanation: 第3段落「Yawning increases blood flow to the head and brings cool air through the mouth, potentially cooling a slightly overheated brain」が根拠。

questions.stem: What is suggested about contagious yawning?
choices: Contagion is stronger between close family/friends, may play a role in group bonding
questions.explanation: 第4段落「contagion is stronger between close family and friends than between strangers, suggesting it may play a role in group bonding」が根拠。

question_data.passage:
There exists a curious paradox at the heart of philosophical inquiry. The questions philosophy addresses—about meaning, ethics, knowledge, the nature of mind—are among the most fundamental that human beings can ask. Yet centuries of dedicated work have produced nothing resembling the cumulative consensus characteristic of natural sciences. Many philosophical questions remain contested today in forms recognizable to thinkers from millennia past. This persistent disagreement suggests something distinctive about philosophy as an intellectual enterprise.

One response holds that philosophical disagreement reflects the genuine difficulty of the questions involved. Some problems—about consciousness, free will, the foundations of morality—may simply lie beyond our cognitive capacities to definitively resolve. On this view, persistent philosophical disagreement is not a sign of failure but of honest engagement with intractable difficulties that easier minds might prematurely close.

Another response, less charitable, holds that philosophy has failed to develop methods adequate to its ambitions. Where sciences have advanced through experimental testing, accumulating evidence, and convergent techniques, philosophy has often relied on argumentation that proves persuasive to those already inclined to accept its conclusions while leaving skeptics unmoved. Without external checks comparable to scientific methods, philosophical positions can persist almost indefinitely without resolution.

A more nuanced view recognizes both responses' partial validity. Some philosophical questions may indeed be resistant to resolution due to their difficulty. Others may have remained open because of methodological limitations that future thinkers might overcome. Still others reveal themselves as confused or pseudo-questions that should not have been raised at all.

What seems clear is that the proper relationship between philosophy and other inquiries deserves continued reflection. Philosophy cannot replace empirical sciences in their domains. Yet sciences cannot answer—and increasingly recognize cannot answer—the framing questions about what their findings mean for human existence. The patient work of philosophy, even without convergent answers, may be essential to taking seriously questions that all human inquiry presupposes but that no single science can fully address.

questions.stem: What paradox does the author identify in philosophy?
choices: Philosophy addresses fundamental questions but produces no cumulative consensus comparable to natural sciences
questions.explanation: 第1段落「centuries of dedicated work have produced nothing resembling the cumulative consensus characteristic of natural sciences」が根拠。

questions.stem: 下線部 'philosophical positions can persist almost indefinitely without resolution.' を日本語に訳しなさい。
questions.answer: 哲学的な立場は、解決を見ないまま、ほとんど無限に持続しうるのである。
questions.explanation: 【東大型】① philosophical positions = 哲学的な立場 (主語) ② can persist = 持続しうる (可能性の助動詞 can) ③ almost indefinitely = ほとんど無限に (副詞句) ④ without resolution = 解決を見ないまま/解決なしに (前置詞 + 名詞)。簡潔だが副詞 almost / indefinitely の正確訳出が要点。

questions.stem: What is the author's overall view of philosophy's value?
choices: Patient philosophical work, even without convergent answers, may be essential for taking seriously questions science cannot fully address
questions.explanation: 最終段落「The patient work of philosophy, even without convergent answers, may be essential to taking seriously questions ... that no single science can fully address」が根拠。
